coredata.dc:title: The heteroaggregation and deposition behavior of nanoplastics on Al2O3 in aquatic environments
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiayan
Liu, Jieyu
Wu, Pingxiao
Sun, Leiye
Chen, Meiqing
Shang, Zhongbo
Ye, Quanyun
Zhu, Nengwu
coredata.dc:description: The ubiquitous Al2O3 is anticipated to interact with nanoplastics, affecting their fate and transport in aquatic environments. In this study, the heteroaggregation and deposition behaviors of polystyrene nanoplastics (PSNPs) on Al2O3 were systematically investigated under different conditions (ionic strength, pH, and natural organic matter). The results showed that significant heteroaggregation occurred between PSNPs and Al2O3 particles under acidic and neutral conditions. When the NaCl concentration was increased from 50 to 500 mM, the heteroaggregation ratio gradually increased. However, poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) inhibited the heteroaggregation of PSNPs-Al2O3 due to steric repulsion. The deposition of PSNPs on Al2O3 surfaces was inhibited as the NaCl concentration or pH values increased. Due to charge reversal and steric repulsion, humic acid (HA) and fulvic acid (FA) prevented the deposition of PSNPs onto Al2O3 surfaces, and the former was more effective in reducing the deposition rate. The interaction mechanism between PSNPs and Al2O3 was revealed by using various characterization techniques and density function theory (DFT) calculation. The results demonstrated that in addition to the dominant electrostatic interaction, there were also weak hydrogen bonds and van der Waals interactions. Our research is of great significance for predicting the migration and fate of PSNPs in aquatic environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoplastics
Al2O3
Heteroaggregation
Deposition
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of NaCl, pH, and NOM
Effect of pH and NOM on deposition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of PSNPs and Al2O3. (a) TEM image of PSNPs. (b) Size distribution histogram of PSNPs. (c) FTIR spectrum of PSNPs and Al2O3. (d) SEM image of Al2O3. (e) XRD spectrum of Al2O3. (f) Zeta potentials of PSNPs and Al2O3 at different solution pH values.

Heteroaggregation of PSNPs on Al2O3. (a) Effect of Al2O3 concentration, CPSNPs = 10.0 mg/L，pH = 6.0 ± 0.5, and T = 298.0 K. (b) Effect of the NaCl concentration, CAl2O3 = 40.0 mg/L, CPSNPs = 10.0 mg/L，pH = 6.0 ± 0.5, and T = 298.0 K. (c) Effect of the solution pH, CAl2O3 = 40.0 mg/L, CPSNPs = 10.0 mg/L, and T = 298.0 K. (d) Effect of the PAA, CAl2O3 = 40.0 mg/L, CPSNPs = 10.0 mg/L, pH = 6.0 ± 0.5, and T = 298.0 K.
Frequency shifts at the third overtone and deposition rates during PSNPs deposits on Al2O3 surfaces under different NaCl concentration. CPSNPs = 10.0 mg/L, pH = 6.0 ± 0.5, and T = 298 K.
Initial deposition rates of PSNPs on Al2O3 surfaces as a function of (a) pH and (b) NOM.

(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XPS analysis before and after heteroaggregation. TEM images of (c) Al2O3-PSNPs heteroaggregation and (d) Al2O3-PSNPs heteroaggregation with PAA. Representative AFM image showing the degree of roughness of (e) bare Al2O3 surfaces and (f) PSNPs on Al2O3 surfaces.

(a) The side view and (b) the top view of the visualized weak interaction regions. (c) The corresponding IGM scatter maps. The Al, O, C, and H are assigned as pink, red, cyan, and white, respectively.

head.ce:title: The heteroaggregation and deposition behavior of nanoplastics on Al2O3 in aquatic environments

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Energy, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou Higher Education Mega Centre

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ubiquitous Al2O3 is anticipated to interact with nanoplastics, affecting their fate and transport in aquatic environments. In this study, the heteroaggregation and deposition behaviors of polystyrene nanoplastics (PSNPs) on Al2O3 were systematically investigated under different conditions (ionic strength, pH, and natural organic matter). The results showed that significant heteroaggregation occurred between PSNPs and Al2O3 particles under acidic and neutral conditions. When the NaCl concentration was increased from 50 to 500 mM, the heteroaggregation ratio gradually increased. However, poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) inhibited the heteroaggregation of PSNPs-Al2O3 due to steric repulsion. The deposition of PSNPs on Al2O3 surfaces was inhibited as the NaCl concentration or pH values increased. Due to charge reversal and steric repulsion, humic acid (HA) and fulvic acid (FA) prevented the deposition of PSNPs onto Al2O3 surfaces, and the former was more effective in reducing the deposition rate. The interaction mechanism between PSNPs and Al2O3 was revealed by using various characterization techniques and density function theory (DFT) calculation. The results demonstrated that in addition to the dominant electrostatic interaction, there were also weak hydrogen bonds and van der Waals interactions. Our research is of great significance for predicting the migration and fate of PSNPs in aquatic environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic pollution has recently emerged in the discussions of scientists, regulators, and the public due to its ubiquity and potential ecological implications (Mitrano et al., 2021; Rochman Chelsea, 2018; Seeley et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). The widespread plastic wastes in lakes, rivers, and oceans as well as soil environment, are anticipated to breakdown into plastic debris and further to the microplastics and nanoplastics, under the effect of mechanical wear (Ekvall et al., 2019), UV degradation (Lambert and Wagner, 2016b), heat (Hernandez et al., 2019), and biological factors (Dawson et al., 2018). Numerous studies predicted that nanoplastics are more abundant in the environment and own stronger toxicity than microplastics (Liu et al., 2021; Tiwari et al., 2020). Although nanoplastics in the environment remained largely unquantified due to a lack of sophisticated tools and techniques, there is no reason to neglect them in various ecosystems (Gigault et al., 2021; Lambert and Wagner, 2016a). Indeed, laboratory-based experiments have already demonstrated that nanoplastics are produced during the fragmentation or weathering of plastic debris (El Hadri et al., 2020; Lambert and Wagner, 2016a). In addition, the discharge of personal care products, such as toothpastes and facial scrubs, was considered as another reason leading to a substantial burden of nanoplastics (Bouwmeester et al., 2015; Hernandez et al., 2017). Therefore, it is crucial to understand the transport of nanoplastics in the environment and further assess their environmental implications and biological risk.

Unlike conventional contaminants such as dissolved organic compounds and heavy metals, the fate and transport of nanoparticles are largely controlled by aggregation and deposition processes (Chen et al., 2021a; Qu et al., 2012). For homoaggregation behaviors, the influence of coexisting cations and natural organic matters (NOM) have been reported (Singh et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021). In addition, numerous studies revealed that nanoplastics could heteroaggregate with environment media (minerals, oxides, and engineering nanoparticles), thus affecting its stability. For example, Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2020) suggested that nanoplastics could adsorb on the iron oxides (goethite, magnetite) by strong electrostatic attraction. Li’s group (Li et al., 2020) revealed that the transport of nanoplastics was influenced by the presence of CeO2 particles. Our previous work also found that the heteroaggregation behaviors between nanoplastics and Mg/Al layered double hydroxides followed the Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek (DLVO) theory (Wu et al., 2022). For deposition kinetics, the quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation monitoring (QCM-D) has been used to characterize the deposition of nanoplastics on the silica surfaces, and the effects of dissolved black carbon and NOM have also been investigated (Shams et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021). These studies suggested that nanoplastics would interact with a variety of environmental components, which could alter their environmental behaviors. However, our understanding of heteroaggregation between nanoplastics and environment media is still fragmentary. Moreover, the deposition behavior of nanoplastics onto environmental surfaces other than SiO2 remains unexplored.

Al2O3 is recognized as one of the most important materials on the earth’s surface (Ren et al., 2014; Shukla and Hill, 2014), thus the interaction between Al2O3 and nanoplastics should not be ignored. On one hand, Al2O3 can be used to model the natural Al-hydroxide phases (e.g., gibbsite) and layers of aluminum oxide in 1:1 clay minerals (e.g., kaolinite), which are widely present in sediments and aqueous environments (Bargar et al., 1997). On the other hand, Al2O3 is a common environmental mediums due to its significant industrial applications, such as abrasive agents, insulators, and catalysts (Janot et al., 2012). However, no specific studies have been performed to depict the heteroaggregation behaviors between nanoplastics and Al2O3 particles, and the deposition behavior of nanoplastics on Al2O3 surfaces also remains unclear. Moreover, the widely distributed NOM will attend the heteroaggregation process between nanoplastics and Al2O3 particles, or affect the deposition behaviors of PSNPs by covering Al2O3 surfaces (Chowdhury et al., 2014b). Therefore, there may be more complicated interactions between nanoplastics and Al2O3 under different environmental conditions.

In this work, the heteroaggregation and deposition behaviors of polystyrene nanoplastics (PSNPs) on Al2O3 under the effects of pH, ionic strength (IS), and NOM were studied, using the UV–vis spectrophotometer and QCM-D equipment, respectively. Poly (acrylic acid) (PAA), humic acid (HA), and fulvic acid (FA) were selected as the representative NOM. Because the presence of PAA does not interfere the UV–vis spectrophotometer detection of PSNPs concentrations and own similar properties as NOM (Ren et al., 2014), while HA and FA are the most important components of NOM (González-Pérez et al., 2004; Ratasuk and Nanny, 2007). Moreover, FTIR, XPS, AFM, TEM, SEM analysis, and DFT calculation were carried out to uncover the interaction mechanisms between PSNPs and Al2O3. The results provide a reference for further research on the fate and transport of nanoplastics in the environment.

The stock suspension of PSNPs (2.5% w/v, 50–100 nm diameter) without surface modification was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD and stored at 4 °C. A final PSNPs working solution of 10 mg/L was prepared by diluting stock solution, which was selected as a representative nanoplastic concentration commonly applied in previous studies. The Al2O3 particles with the size of about 1.4 µm were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. The background solutions were prepared by adding a defined amount of analytical grade NaCl into deionized water, and the pH values were adjusted by 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH. Then, the background solutions were filtered through a membrane filter (0.22 µm) for subsequent experiments. PAA, HA, and FA were used as the representative NOM in aquatic environments and also purchased from Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

The morphology and physicochemical properties of PSNPs and Al2O3 were characterized by TEM, SEM, FTIR, XRD, and Zeta potentials analysis. The FTIR, XPS, AFM, TEM, SEM were carried out to reveal the interaction mechanisms between PSNPs and Al2O3. More details about characterization were provided in supporting Information (SI) Text S1.

The heteroaggregation experiments of PSNPs on Al2O3 particles were conducted in 50 mL vials by the batch technique. Briefly, Al2O3 suspension was prepared by suspending Al2O3 particles in water followed by sonicating for 15 min. Then, PSNPs suspension, NaCl solution, and PAA solution were added to achieve the designed concentrations. Negligible volumes of 0.1 M HCl or NaOH solution was used to adjust the suspension pH values. The mixture kept stirring at a speed of 500 rpm for 5 h and Al2O3 was separated completely from the aqueous solution by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min with an interval of 30 or 60 min. After that, the supernatants (3 mL) were moved to a quartz cuvette to examine the absorbance at the wavelength of 250 nm using the UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent-2600, USA). According to the blank tests, the aggregation of PSNPs on the vial wall was negligible. The normalized heteroaggregation curves were plotted with the normalized (C/C0) against the heteroaggregation time (t). All the experimental data were the average of duplicate determinations.

The deposition kinetics of PSNPs onto Al2O3 surfaces in aquatic systems were studied using a quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation monitoring equipment (QCM-D, E4, Q-sense, Biolin Scientific, Sweden). Deposition rates were measured under various IS and pH conditions, as well as the presence of NOM (HA and FA). Prior to each determination, aluminum oxide sensors (Al2O3, QSX 309) surfaces were pre-cleaned according to the recommendations from Q-sense. The cleaning procedures were outlined in full in Text S2. The Al2O3 sensors were first equilibrated with deionized water to achieve initial stabilization by monitoring the frequency and dissipation signal at the third overtone (f (3) and D (3), respectively) at the beginning. Subsequently, the Al2O3 crystal surfaces were rinsed with background solutions until stabilized. Finally, the suspension of PSNPs with the same IS, pH, or NOM concentration was injected into the flow chambers for at least 20 min. During the entire deposition experiment, the temperature of the chamber was maintained at 25 ± 0.2 ℃. A peristaltic pump was used to keep the flow of all suspensions or solutions constant at 0.15 mL/min, and frequency shifts were continually recorded. The overtone frequencies of crystal sensors’ decrease with the deposition, as following the Sauerbrey’s equation (Chowdhury et al., 2014a; Fatisson et al., 2009).
(1)
∆ m = −
C ∆
f
n
n
Where Δm is the deposited mass, Δf is the shift in overtone frequency, n is the overtone number (n = 3 in this study), and C is the crystal constant (17.7 ng/Hz·cm2 for the 5 MHz crystal). Initial deposition rates r f are defined as rates of frequency shift in a time period t, respectively:
(2)
r
f
=
d ∆
f
( 3 )
dt

To better understand the interaction mechanism between PSNPs and Al2O3, the Independent Gradient Mode (IGM) analysis was performed. IGM analysis is a very novel method that investigates non-covalent interactions by detecting the reduced electron density gradient that comes from the electron density and its first derivative (Lefebvre et al., 2017; Lu and Chen, 2013). Multiwfn and Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) software were used to perform the related interaction analysis and figure plotting (Lu and Chen, 2012). Geometric optimization was performed through Materials Studio 2019 and Gaussian 09. The detailed information was listed in Text S3 of the SI.

TEM and SEM were used to examine the morphology of PSNPs and Al2O3. As illustrated in Fig. 1a and b, PSNPs were spherical with smooth surfaces and a particle size of around 100 nm. Al2O3 presented an irregular shape with partial aggregation, and the mean size was about 1.38 µm (Fig. 1d and Fig. S1). The FTIR spectra of PSNPs (Fig. 1c) illustrated the benzene ring vibration (680, 757 and 3026 cm−1) (Tiwari et al., 2020), the aromatic C-H (1453, 1493 cm−1) (Shams et al., 2020), the CC stretch of the aromatic ring (1602 cm−1) and aliphatic C-H deformation respectively (2923 cm−1) (Liu et al., 2019). As for Al2O3, the strong peaks at 876 and 598 cm−1 were attributed to the Al-O lattice vibration (Cui et al., 2020). The bounded and isolated hydroxyl groups, and adsorbed water molecules groups were assigned to the characteristic peak at 3442 cm−1 (Fatisson et al., 2009; Wu et al., 2020). In Fig. 1e, the diffraction peaks of Al2O3 at 2θ positions 25.6°, 37.8°, 43.4°, and 59.8° matched well with the (012), (110), (113), and (211) lattice planes, respectively, corresponding to the standard card of Al2O3 (JCPDS No. 75–1863). The narrow, strong diffraction peaks indicated that Al2O3 had highly crystalline structures (Liu et al., 2017b). The zeta potentials of PSNPs and Al2O3 were examined as a function of the solution pH (Fig. 1f). At the whole pH range (2.0–10.0), PSNPs were all negatively charged. Al2O3 remained positively charged over the pH rang 2.0–8.5, and turned negatively when pH over 8.5. The point of zero charges (pHzpc) of the Al2O3 was approximately 8.5.

Fig. 2a presented the changes in concentration of PSNPs under the effect of Al2O3 at pH 6.0 ± 0.5. It was observed that PSNPs maintained high stability in the absence of Al2O3. The generally threshold of absolute zeta-potential for a stable colloidal suspension is over 30 mV (Liu et al., 2017a). As could be seen in Fig. 1f, the zeta-potential of PNSPs was approximately − 40 mV at pH 6.0. Therefore, the surface potential could offer sufficient electrostatic repulsion to prevent PSNPs homoaggregation. However, the stability of PSNPs was broken in the presence of Al2O3, and this destabilizing ability was strongly associated with the Al2O3 concentration (10 mg/L-80 mg/L). This certainly indicated that Al2O3 particles were able to heteroaggregate with PSNPs and affect their transportation. The heteroaggregation could occur because Al2O3 and PSNPs had the opposite surface charges (23.2 mV and −38.3 mV) at pH 6.0, resulting in a significant electrostatic interaction. Previous works also found that heteroaggregation could occur between positively charged PSNPs and negatively charged minerals, such as LDH and iron oxides, due to electrostatic attraction (Chen et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2019; Tiwari et al., 2020).

To better understand the heteroaggregation mechanism and rate-limiting steps, the kinetic result of PSNPs under 40 mg/L Al2O3 condition was modeled by three common kinetic models, including pseudo-first-order models (PFO), pseudo-second-order models (PSO), and the intra-particle diffusion models (IPD) (Singh et al., 2020), as shown in Fig. S2. The corresponding equations and fitted parameters of kinetic models were summarized in Text S4 and Table S1. The correlation coefficient R2 was found to be higher in PSO (0.998) as compared to that for PFO (0.990), and the <chi>2 value of PSO (0.002) was less than PFO (0.013). In addition, the sorption capacity calculated by PSO was 4.29 mg/g, which agreed well with the experimentally ascertained value. The good fitting indicated that the sorption of PSNPs on Al2O3 followed PSO mode, and the heteroaggregation behavior was controlled by multiple processes, such as external liquid film diffusion, surface adsorption and particle internal diffusion. In order to understand the rate controlling factors of PSNPs sorption onto Al2O3, the IPD model was applied to fit the experimental data. Obviously, the straight line could not be well fitted, indicating it was not a simple diffusion mode. Through IPD modeling, the current data revealed three linear portions corresponding to membrane diffusion, IPD, and equilibration stages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NaCl, pH, and NOM
ce:section.ce:para: The heteroaggregation extents of PSNPs on Al2O3 under various NaCl concentration conditions were shown in Fig. 2b. In theory, the increased IS would reduce the absolute zeta-potential values of PSNPs and Al2O3 as a result of the compression effect from the electrical double layer, restraining the attachment between PSNPs and Al2O3 (Lin et al., 2006). However, a higher heteroaggregation ratio was discovered at higher IS solution condition. This enhanced amount of heteroaggregation was attributed to the dominant homoaggregation phenomenon of PSNPs caused by charge screening, then the homoaggregated PSNPs particles adhered to Al2O3 surfaces. Fig. S3 dominated that the increased NaCl concentration could promote the homoaggregation of PSNPs, and this result was also evidenced by DLVO interaction energy profile calculation (Text S5) and hydrodynamic radius measurement in our previous work (Wu et al., 2022).

The solution pH plays an important role in colloidal stability and aggregation behavior. As shown in Fig. 2c, the heteroaggregation was strongly influenced by solution pH values. At pH 3.0–7.0, the positively charged Al2O3 attached to negatively charged PSNPs (Fig. 1f) through electrostatic attraction (Li et al., 2020) and the strongest heteroaggregation was detected at pH 3.0. However, increased solution pH resulted in considerable reduction in heteroaggregation. In particular, PSNPs were difficult to heteroaggregate on Al2O3 at pH 9.0. This was the reason that PSNPs became more negatively charged (−63 mV) at pH 9.0 (Fig. 1f), causing strong electrostatic repulsion to prevent their homoaggregate and become a more stable suspension (Hierrezuelo et al., 2010). Furthermore, the surface charge of Al2O3 and PSNPs were all negative (Fig. 1f), resulting in larger repulsion and energy barrier between them, which prevent their heteroaggregation greatly. These findings confirmed the importance of electrostatic force in the heteroaggregation between PSNPs and Al2O3.

PAA has a simple structure and similar properties with the NOM (Ren et al., 2014; Sadeghpour et al., 2011), and the presence of PAA did not interfere with the UV–vis spectrophotometer detection of PSNPs concentration (Fig. S4). As a result, we selected the PAA as a representative NOM to investigate its impact on PSNPs-Al2O3 heteroaggregation. As could be seen in Fig. 2d, PSNPs became stable in Al2O3 suspension with different concentrations of PAA. On one hand, PAA could be adsorbed onto PSNPs via hydrophobic interactions and prevented their homoaggregation as a result of the steric hindrance effect and more negative charge (Ren et al., 2014). Cai (Cai et al., 2018) also found that the zeta potentials of PSNPs in the presence of NOMs (SRHA and SRFA) became more negative and the energy barrier heights of PSNPs increased. On the other hand, PAA competed with PSNPs for the limited active sites on Al2O3 surfaces and formed complexes, further reducing the heteroaggregation between PSNPs and Al2O3. A similar conclusion was also obtained in a previous study of the hematite-PAA system, which demonstrated that the negatively charged PAA would tend to collapse at the positively charged hematite surfaces because of the strong electrostatic attraction (Zhang et al., 1999). This result further confirmed that NOM had a stabilizing effect on PSNPs in the natural aquatic environment.

Fig. 3 showed the frequency shifts and deposition rates of PSNPs on the Al2O3 surfaces, as a function of NaCl concentration. It was obvious that deposition rates of PSNPs on Al2O3 surfaces remained relatively consistent with increased NaCl concentration from 0 to 50 mM, because the deposition of PSNPs on the Al2O3 surfaces was favorable and further charge screening caused by increased IS couldn’t promote deposition. However, with the further increase of NaCl concentration above 50 mM, the deposition rates began to decline, which was the reason that the diffuse double layer was strongly compressed, and the electrostatic attraction between PSNPs and Al2O3 surfaces was attenuated. Meanwhile, further increase of IS compressed the double layer of PSNPs and promoted its homoaggregation (Fig. S3), resulting in a decreased diffusion rate of PSNPs on the deposition surfaces (Fatisson et al., 2009). In a prior work, a similar trend of deposition rates for graphene oxide on the Al2O3 surfaces was also reported (Chowdhury et al., 2014b). Overall, the deposition behavior of PSNPs on bare Al2O3 surfaces would be affected by IS in aquatic environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and NOM on deposition
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a showed the deposition rates of PSNPs on the Al2O3 surfaces under different pH values, and the detailed frequency shifts were provided in Fig. S5. At pH 3.0 and pH 6.0, attractive electrostatic forces predominate because the Al2O3 surfaces are positively charged while the PSNPs are negatively charged. Thus, significant deposition of PSNPs on the Al2O3 surfaces was observed, with the initial deposition rate being 6.538 and 5.6288 Hz/min, respectively. At pH 9.0, both PSNPs and Al2O3 were negatively charged, showing negligible deposition due to electrostatic repulsion. These results were similar to those in the process of heteroaggregation, further confirming that electrostatic force played a significant part in the interaction between PSNPs and Al2O3.

Most studies implied that the widely existing NOM in the environment could significantly influence the deposition of nanoparticles (Huangfu et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2012). In order to explore the influence of NOM on the deposition of PSNPs on Al2O3 surfaces, we explored the co-deposition behavior of PSNPs and representative NOM (HA and FA) on Al2O3 surfaces, as well as the deposition of PSNPs on HA/FA-coated Al2O3 surfaces, and the results were shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. S6. Compared with bare Al2O3, the initial deposition rates of PSNPs decreased dramatically with the addition of 3 mg/L HA or FA (0–1.74 Hz/min). It was the reason that the HA or FA molecules could absorb on PSNPs surfaces, leading to the significant steric repulsion between PSNPs and Al2O3, and therefore reduced the deposition efficiency and strongly enhanced the stability of PSNPs (Xu et al., 2021). Besides, negligible PSNPs deposition on HA/FA-coated Al2O3 surfaces was observed, because NOM is negatively charged in the natural environment, which could absorb on Al2O3 surfaces induced its charge inversion, resulting in an electrostatically unfavorable condition for PSNPs deposition on NOM-coated Al2O3 surfaces. In addition, NOM coatings might produce strong steric interactions, but non-uniform or incomplete surface coverage might also create compressible nanoscale roughness, reducing the energy barrier height and the magnitude of the primary minimum (Bradford et al., 2021). However, with the decreases of separation distance, strong steric repulsion would dominate the interaction energy profile and prevent the deposition. Furthermore, HA and FA with low molecular weight and relatively high flexibility were not expected to cause bridging flocculation (Zhang et al., 1999). In summary, both co-existed NOM and NOM-coated surfaces could inhibit the deposition of PSNPs.

The FTIR, XPS, TEM, and AFM analyses were conducted to examine the chemical structure and morphology changes during the heteroaggregation and deposition process. As could be seen in Fig. 5a, several new peaks were found at Al2O3-PSNPs heteroaggregate compared to bare Al2O3 in FTIR spectra. The adsorption peaks at 1453 and 1493 cm−1 should be attributed to the aromatic C-H structure of PSNPs (Lu et al., 2018), whereas aliphatic C-H deformation and the benzene ring vibration of PSNP are reflected in the characteristic absorption peaks at 2923 cm−1 and 3026 cm−1 (Shams et al., 2020). These results suggested that PSNPs had attached onto Al2O3 particles. Moreover, the surface element analysis of XPS (Fig. 5d) showed that the content of C elements enhanced greatly after heteroaggregation with PSNPs, as the reason that PSNPs had a higher content of C (93.72%) than that of Al2O3 (19.85%), further confirmed the successful formation of Al2O3-PSNPs complexes.

TEM was employed to investigate the heteroaggregation of Al2O3-PSNPs with and without the presence of PAA. Fig. 5c clearly showed that great amounts of PSNPs were attached to the edges of Al2O3 and form large complexes. However, PAA could prevent the homoaggregation of PSNPs and the heteroaggregation between Al2O3 and PSNPs, resulting in higher dispersity of PSNPs and Al2O3 and increasing stability of the binary systems drastically (Fig. 5d). These results were generally consistent with the above-mentioned conclusion of heteroaggregation experiments.

AFM images of the Al2O3 surfaces before and after deposition of PSNPs were shown in Fig. 5e and f. The bare Al2O3 surfaces exhibited a uniform distribution with features less than 7 nm in height (Fig. 5e). Compared to featureless bare Al2O3 surfaces, some randomly distributed bright dots were seen in Fig. 5f, and the height of the Al2O3 surfaces after deposition by PSNPs was about 100 nm. According to the SEM analysis, PSNP has a spherical shape with the average diameter of 100 nm. Obviously, these results indicated that PSNPs had deposited on the Al2O3 surfaces, with the monolayer deposition mode, which were also supported by the Fig. S7 and QCM-D experiments.

The probable interaction mechanism between PSNPs and Al2O3 was investigated further using DFT theoretical calculation. Non-covalent interactions include van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds, and they cover a wide range of attraction and repulsion forces between atoms or molecules (Lefebvre et al., 2017). Color codes associated to electron density derivatives distinguish the type of the interaction in this model: red for strongly repulsive, green for van der Waals forces, and blue for strongly attractive interactions (Zhu et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 6a and b, the bright green isosurface revealed that the van der Waals forces were important in the interaction between PSNPs and Al2O3. In addition, the C-H group of benzene could form a weak H-bond (C-H…O) with the oxygen atom of Al2O3 (Dawley et al., 2012), thus the isosurface showed blue color. Every point in the colorful scatter maps corresponded to a grid point in 3D space. Fig. 6c showed that the δginter had a noticeable peak where (λ
2 ) ρ was around − 0.035 a.u., indicating the formation of the hydrogen bond (Kang et al., 2015). In a word, except for the electrostatic interaction, IGM analysis revealed that van der Waals forces and hydrogen bond interactions also affected the interaction between PSNPs and Al2O3.

It is crucial to figure out how Al2O3 effects the transport of nanoplastics in natural environments. Thus, the heteroaggregation and deposition behaviors of PSNPs in the presence of Al2O3 were investigated using a UV–vis spectrophotometer and QCM-D techniques, respectively. We found that PSNPs could heteroaggregate with Al2O3 particles and deposited on Al2O3 surfaces significantly. As the pH increased, both the heteroaggregation and deposition were inhibited due to the electrostatic repulsion. Interestingly, high NaCl concentration promoted the heteroaggregation of PSNPs but reduced its deposition on the Al2O3 surfaces. The coexisted NOM (PAA, HA, and FA) could prevent the heteroaggregation and deposition of PSNPs on Al2O3 and enhance the stability and mobility of PSNPs. Moreover, our data suggested that NOM coatings induced electrostatic and steric repulsive energies, which presented an unfavorable Al2O3 surfaces for PSNPs deposition and further decreased the retention of PSNPs. Finally, TEM, AFM, FTIR, XPS analysis, and DFT theoretical calculations were combined to reveal the interaction mechanism, suggesting that electrostatic interaction, weak hydrogen bond, and van der Waals interaction are the main force controlling the heteroaggregation and deposition behaviors of PSNPs. In summary, our study innovative combined the UV–vis spectrophotometer, QCM-D technology, DLVO theory and DFT calculations to explore the interaction between PSNPs and Al2O3 in a more depth way, highlighted the impact of IS, pH and NOMs on nanoplastics heteroaggregation and deposition behaviors, which could provide a profound insight into their actual behavior in aquatic environments.

Jiayan Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing − original draft preparation. Jieyu Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing − review & editing. Pingxiao Wu: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing. Leiye Sun: Visualization, Resources. Meiqing Chen: Visualization, Resources. Zhongbo Shang: Writing − review & editing. Quanyun Ye: Writing − review & editing, Investigation. Nengwu Zhu: Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42172042, 41972037), the Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation of Guangdong Province (2019B1515120015, 2021A1515110371), the Guangdong Special Support Program for Local Innovative and Research Teams Project (2019BT02L218), the Guangzhou Science and Technology Program (202206010055), the Guangdong Science and Technology Program (2020B121201003), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, SCUT (2020ZYGXZR070).

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoplastics have raised global concerns because of their possible ecotoxicological impacts and high exposure potential. Al2O3 is abundant in soil and water environments, and it is anticipated to interact with nanoplastics, affecting their transport and fate in aquatic environments. Our research sheds light on the heteroaggregation behavior of polystyrene nanoplastics (PSNPs) with Al2O3 particles, as well as the deposition process of PSNPs on Al2O3 surfaces. This is of great significance for predicting the migration and environmental fate of PSNPs in aquatic environments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.128964.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid removal of toxic metals Cu2+ and Pb2+ by amino trimethylene phosphonic acid intercalated layered double hydroxide: A combined experimental and DFT study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, S., Asim Khan, M., Wang, F., Bano, Z. and Xia, M. (2020) Rapid removal of toxic metals Cu2+ and Pb2+ by amino trimethylene phosphonic acid intercalated layered double hydroxide: A combined experimental and DFT study. Chem Eng J 392, 123711.
